text:
“10. Protection provided under Section 53-A of 
the Act to the proposed transferee is a shield 
only against the transferor. It disentitles the 
transferor from disturbing the possession of the 
proposed transferee who is put in possession 
in pursuance to such an agreement. It has 
nothing to do with the ownership of the proposed 
transferor who remains full owner of the 
property till it is legally conveyed by executing a 
registered sale deed in favour of the transferee. 
Such a right to protect possession against the 
proposed vendor cannot be pressed into service 
against a third party.”
18. It is thus clear that a transfer of immovable property 
by way of sale can only be by a deed of conveyance 
(sale deed). In the absence of a deed of conveyance 
(duly stamped and registered as required by law), no 
right, title or interest in an immovable property can be 
transferred.
